text: This is a instructional video on a da Vinci XI right inguinal hernia repair. I will be focused on performing a safe and thorough dissection.

The best way to ensure that your dissection is safe and thorough is to start with good port placement. I like to place the ports in the upper abdomen, which means the camera is usually not in the location of the umbilicus. The reason for this is because the umbilicus can vary in height relative to the pelvis. In some patients, as in the illustration shown, the umbilicus is very low.

The surgeon makes a peritoneal incision at the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), highlighting the challenges of workspace and flap closure when the camera is positioned at the umbilicus in patients with a low umbilicus.

The surgeon positions the camera port twelve to fifteen centimeters above the level of the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), aligning with the peritoneal incision level. The camera port is placed slightly off midline to enter through the rectus complex, aiming to minimize the risk of a midline incisional hernia.

The surgeon positions the scissor and grasper ports at the same level as the camera port, approximately eight centimeters away. The optical entry is made to the right of midline for a right inguinal hernia repair, with the surgeon standing on the patient's left side.

The surgeon places the first robotic port in the right upper quadrant, with the assistant positioned on the patient's right side. After inserting the port, the assistant maneuvers the camera to aid in placing the left upper quadrant port and replacing the initial camera port. Local anesthetic is infiltrated at the skin level, and a rectus sheath block or mini tap block is performed adjacent to the port site.

The surgeon places the robot port into the abdominal wall until the dark band is buried within the muscle. The camera is positioned at the right upper quadrant port, and the left upper quadrant port is placed next, spaced approximately eight centimeters apart. The surgeon infiltrates the rectus and retro-rectus space, ensuring the port is inserted until the dark band is within the muscle, positioning the remote center within the abdominal wall.

The surgeon upsizes the initial optical trocar to an eight-millimeter robot port. The mesh and suture are placed at the beginning of the procedure to maintain efficiency during instrument exchanges. A tap block or angle of nerve block is performed on the operating side as the laparoscope is inserted.

The robot is docked at the pelvis, utilizing a zero-degree scope for optimal orientation. A fenestrated bipolar instrument is positioned in the left hand, while a monopolar curved scissor is placed in the right hand. The surgeon releases tension by burping the ports before proceeding to the patient.

The surgeon begins the dissection lateral to the linea semilunaris, firmly grasping the peritoneum and making an incision. A significant amount of yellow preperitoneal fat is visible, providing protection from the abdominal wall. As the dissection progresses medially, the amount of preperitoneal fat decreases. The surgeon seeks the areolar plane between the transverse salus fascia and the thin preperitoneal fascia, which is closely adherent to the peritoneum. The dissection continues through the areolar tissue, with minimal vessels visible in the plane. The surgeon identifies the space between the yellow and orange fat layers.

The surgeon uses robotic assistance to work laterally, elevating fibroareolar tissue to access deep layers beneath the preperitoneal fascia. The robotic precision and magnification reveal multiple unnamed fascial layers adjacent to the peritoneum.

The surgeon identifies and incises the Shiozaki layer, allowing the displacement of surrounding fat. Subsequently, the Azure layer is incised, revealing the testicular vessels and peritoneum. The testicular vessels are carefully separated from the peritoneum, establishing a safe plane between these structures.

The surgeon dissects the fibroareolar tissue, identified as transverse salus fascia, from the peritoneum, focusing on the area encircling the vas deferens and testicular vessels. The transverse salus fascia is incised to facilitate the removal of the hernia sac. Cauterization is performed safely between ten and two o'clock, avoiding the posterior vas deferens and testicular vessels. The surgeon uses a critical maneuver to unstack the structures by pulling with the left hand to the nine o'clock position and scraping with the right hand to the three to four o'clock position.

The surgeon uses quick bursts of cautery to achieve hemostasis while following the plane presented by the areolar tissue. Fat is left against the rectus muscle, and the recommendation is to move anterior to the fat to directly approach the rectus muscle, descending onto the pubis and Cooper's ligament to minimize the risk of entering the bladder.

The surgeon dissects in the plane where tissues separate cleanly from the pubis and Cooper's ligament, ensuring the contralateral rectus muscle is visible. The dissection extends two to three centimeters past the midline and deep to Cooper's ligament, maintaining hemostasis to clearly identify critical structures like the bladder.

The surgeon continues pulling tissue to the nine o'clock position and stripping to the three o'clock position to locate the peritoneal edge. This maneuver reveals the vas deferens, which is carefully avoided to prevent injury. The dissection occurs in a challenging area near the bladder, iliac vein, and vas deferens, with a focus on maintaining hemostasis and identifying natural areolar separations between femoral fat and bladder fat.

The surgeon uses quick, short bursts of cautery to maintain hemostasis during dissection. The vas deferens is identified, guiding the surgeon to the correct dissection plane. The natural planes between the fat and critical structures become visible as the dissection progresses.

The surgeon gently pulls and pushes through the fatty tissue, using cautery to identify the developing areolar plane. The peritoneum is carefully peeled off the testicular vessels and vas deferens, a process known as parietalizing the cord, ensuring the mesh does not sit on the peritoneum to prevent posterior recurrence. Hemostasis is maintained to clearly visualize critical structures such as the iliac vein, vas deferens, and testicular vessels.

The surgeon continues the posterior dissection to achieve a mesh overlap of four to five centimeters posteriorly and laterally. Hemostasis reveals the peritoneal edge, which is carefully stripped off the testicular vessels and vas deferens. Fibroareolar tissue is left over the lateral pelvic wall to protect the femoral branch, the general branch of the genitofemoral nerve, and the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. The width of the mesh is assessed to ensure the pocket size is adequate.

The completed dissection reveals adequate space for the mesh to extend past the midline to the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS), and two centimeters beyond Cooper's ligament. The mesh can be tucked between the bladder and pubis in the space of Retzius, while the transversalis fascia or fibroareolar tissue remains undisturbed laterally.

The surgeon searches for a core lipoma but does not find one, pulling on the fat to locate the ilipubic tract. The medial umbilical ligament is identified as it connects to the iliac vein. The mesh is tucked into the retropubic space to prevent the bladder from lifting it, ensuring it lays flat against the left rectus muscle. The scissor is replaced with a mega suture cut needle driver.

The surgeon places a pro grip mesh, ensuring it extends past the midline from left to right. The mesh is initially folded for easier deployment, and unfolded flap by flap at the crease. The seam of the anatomical mesh is positioned at or just below the level of Cooper's ligament and approximately one to two centimeters posterior to the internal ring.

The surgeon unfolds the upper flap of the mesh, ensuring the lower flap is completely open. The mesh is positioned to avoid the peritoneum sitting behind it, and is tucked between the superior pubic rami and the bladder. The mesh is then secured in place, with adjustments made to prevent the peritoneum from being too close.

The surgeon closes the peritoneal flap using a six-inch 3-0 V-Lock suture, starting from lateral to medial where the flap edges are closest. The lower flap appears very thin, requiring careful handling to prevent tearing. The left hand assists by pulling the needle out and presenting it to the right hand for efficient suturing.

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the needle, using the left hand to present it to the right hand for efficient stitching. The needle is parked for easy re-grabbing if necessary. The surgeon performs three to four throws before pulling the suture through, ensuring there are no gaps between the sutures.

The surgeon ends the suture at the medial umbilical ligament, ensuring the suture is protected. The suture is locked and cut flush against the peritoneum. Any visible peritoneal holes are repaired with a V-lock suture. In cases where the peritoneum appears thin, a figure-of-eight suture technique is used to prevent tearing.
The surgeon completes the repair by using figure-eight sutures with a V-lock technique, ensuring secure closure of the surgical site.
